enrichment.short_description: Enterprise digital experience analytics and continuous product design platform combining session replay, journey analytics, and AI-driven insight generation to help product, UX, and engineering teams find and fix friction in web and mobile experiences.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom enterprise pricing based on the number of digital properties monitored, session and data volume, and service level; no self-serve public plans are published.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing only - contact sales for a quote
enrichment.seo_title: Quantum Metric Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Quantum Metric costs, who it is built for, and how its session replay and Felix AI features compare before you buy. Independent 2026 breakdown.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Quantum Metric is an enterprise digital experience analytics and continuous product design (CPD) platform founded in 2015 by CEO Mario Ciabarra and headquartered in Colorado Springs, Colorado. The company grew out of Ciabarra's earlier work in web performance and analytics at Akamai and Newegg, and it built Quantum Metric to give product, engineering, UX, and business teams a single quantified view of how real users experience websites and mobile apps. The platform combines quantitative behavioral analytics (funnels, journeys, conversion and revenue impact) with qualitative evidence (full session replay, DOM-accurate reconstruction, and friction/anomaly detection) so teams can both see what happened and understand why. In recent years Quantum Metric has layered generative and agentic AI on top of this data through a feature set called Felix, which offers an Analyst mode for automated insight summarization, an Agent mode that can take or recommend action, and a Partner mode for deeper collaborative analysis. Quantum Metric reached unicorn status in January 2021 after a 200 million dollar Series B round led by Insight Partners, pushing its valuation above 1 billion dollars; total disclosed funding is roughly 250 million dollars across seed, Series A, and Series B rounds plus venture debt. The company reports roughly 500 employees and has been recognized as an Inc. Best Workplace. Quantum Metric is used primarily by large, digitally intensive enterprises in retail, travel, financial services, telecom, and healthcare that need to quantify the business impact of digital friction at scale, often processing very high session volumes across many web and mobile properties. Pricing is entirely custom and undisclosed publicly; it scales with the number of digital properties monitored, session and data volume, and the level of customer success and professional services included, with add-ons available for employee experience monitoring, data enrichment, contact center integration, and streaming data to external warehouses. Because pricing requires a sales conversation and implementation typically involves tagging and configuration work, Quantum Metric is generally positioned against other enterprise digital experience and product analytics platforms such as Contentsquare, FullStory, and Glassbox rather than lightweight, self-serve web analytics tools. Independent review platforms including G2, Gartner Peer Insights, and Capterra list Quantum Metric among top-rated enterprise digital experience monitoring and session replay products, with users frequently citing session replay depth, cross-team alignment on a single source of truth, and integration with existing analytics stacks as key strengths, while cost and implementation complexity are the most commonly cited drawbacks for smaller organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Quantum Metric is an enterprise-grade digital experience analytics platform that unifies quantitative behavioral data with qualitative session replay so that product, UX, engineering, and business teams can work from a single, quantified view of the customer experience. Founded in 2015 by Mario Ciabarra and based in Colorado Springs, Colorado, the company built its platform around the idea that most organizations struggle because different teams measure success with different, disconnected tools.

Since reaching a valuation above 1 billion dollars in 2021, Quantum Metric has expanded from a pure analytics and replay tool into what it now calls a continuous product design platform, adding agentic AI capabilities under the Felix brand that summarize sessions, surface anomalies automatically, and recommend or trigger actions across connected systems. The platform is aimed squarely at large enterprises running high-traffic digital properties where even small conversion or performance issues carry significant revenue impact.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Quantum Metric captures full, DOM-accurate session replay alongside structured behavioral data, letting teams both watch exactly what a user experienced and quantify how common or costly that experience was across the whole user base. Journey and funnel visualization, interaction heatmaps, and automated anomaly detection help teams spot friction points such as rage clicks, form abandonment, or error states without manually reviewing thousands of sessions.

The Felix AI layer adds an Analyst mode that automatically summarizes patterns across sessions, an Agent mode that can act on identified issues, and a Partner mode intended for deeper collaborative investigation. Additional capabilities include real-time data activation to downstream systems, PII redaction during implementation, employee experience monitoring, contact center integrations, and data streaming to customer-owned data warehouses for teams that want to combine Quantum Metric data with their own analytics infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Quantum Metric does not publish self-serve pricing tiers or a starting price. Instead, every customer receives a custom quote based on the number of websites, mobile apps, and other digital properties they want monitored, the volume of session and event data collected, and the level of customer success and professional services bundled into the contract.

This enterprise sales-led pricing model is typical of large-scale digital experience and session replay platforms and reflects Quantum Metric's focus on large organizations rather than small businesses or individual developers. Prospective buyers should expect a scoping conversation with the Quantum Metric sales team, and total cost can vary significantly depending on traffic volume, number of properties, data retention needs, and optional add-ons like employee experience monitoring or contact center integration.

features.title: Full session replay
features.description: DOM-accurate reconstruction of real user sessions across web and mobile so teams can see exactly what a customer experienced, not just aggregate metrics.

features.title: Journey and funnel analytics
features.description: Visualizes how users move through key flows and where they drop off, tying friction directly to conversion and revenue impact.

features.title: Automated anomaly and friction detection
features.description: Surfaces issues like rage clicks, error states, and abandoned forms automatically instead of requiring manual session review.

features.title: Felix agentic AI
features.description: AI-driven Analyst, Agent, and Partner modes that summarize sessions, surface insights, and can recommend or take action on identified issues.

features.title: Real-time data activation
features.description: Pushes Quantum Metric insights and events into other business systems in real time to trigger downstream workflows.

features.title: Employee experience monitoring
features.description: Extends the same session-level analytics approach to internal, employee-facing applications as an add-on.

features.title: Contact center integration
features.description: Connects digital session data with contact center interactions to help support teams resolve issues faster with full context.

features.title: Data warehouse streaming
features.description: Streams raw Quantum Metric data into customer-owned data warehouses for custom analysis alongside other business data.

pros: Very deep, DOM-accurate session replay combined with quantified behavioral data in one platform
Enterprise-grade scalability suited to very high session volumes and many digital properties
Felix AI features reduce manual analysis time by auto-surfacing anomalies and insights
Strong fit for regulated, high-traffic industries like retail, travel, and financial services
Real-time data activation lets other systems react to Quantum Metric findings automatically
Recognized as a strong workplace and well regarded in enterprise analyst and review platforms
cons: No published pricing; every deal requires a custom sales quote
Cost and scope generally make it impractical for small businesses or low-traffic sites
Implementation and tagging setup can take meaningful time before value is realized
Feature depth creates a learning curve for teams new to session-replay-driven analytics
Some advanced capabilities like employee experience or contact center integration are paid add-ons
Best suited to organizations that already have dedicated product or UX analytics teams

faqs.question: What is Quantum Metric used for
faqs.answer: Quantum Metric is used by enterprises to combine quantitative analytics with session replay so product, UX, and engineering teams can identify and quantify friction in digital experiences and prioritize fixes by business impact.

faqs.question: How much does Quantum Metric cost
faqs.answer: Quantum Metric does not publish pricing. Cost is custom and depends on the number of digital properties monitored, session and data volume, and the service level included in the contract.

faqs.question: Who founded Quantum Metric
faqs.answer: Quantum Metric was founded in 2015 by Mario Ciabarra and is headquartered in Colorado Springs, Colorado.

faqs.question: What is Felix in Quantum Metric
faqs.answer: Felix is Quantum Metric's agentic AI layer, offering Analyst mode for automated insight summarization, Agent mode for taking or recommending action, and Partner mode for deeper collaborative analysis.

faqs.question: Is Quantum Metric a unicorn company
faqs.answer: Yes. Quantum Metric raised a 200 million dollar Series B round in January 2021 that valued the company above 1 billion dollars, and it has raised roughly 250 million dollars in total disclosed funding.

faqs.question: How is Quantum Metric different from Google Analytics
faqs.answer: Quantum Metric focuses on session-level replay and quantified friction detection for enterprise product and UX teams, whereas Google Analytics is primarily a traffic and marketing analytics tool without native full session replay.

faqs.question: Does Quantum Metric require developer implementation
faqs.answer: Yes, initial setup typically involves tagging digital properties and configuring PII redaction, and Quantum Metric's team provides implementation support as part of enterprise contracts.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial of Quantum Metric
faqs.answer: Quantum Metric is sold through a sales-led enterprise process rather than public self-serve signup, so prospective customers should contact sales to discuss a trial or proof of concept.

use_cases.title: Ecommerce conversion optimization
use_cases.description: Retail and ecommerce teams use session replay and journey analytics to find and fix checkout friction, abandoned carts, and broken flows that suppress conversion.
use_cases.audience: Ecommerce product managers and conversion rate optimization teams

use_cases.title: Enterprise UX and product friction remediation
use_cases.description: Large product organizations use quantified friction detection to prioritize engineering fixes based on measurable revenue and experience impact rather than anecdote.
use_cases.audience: Product managers, UX researchers, and engineering leads at large enterprises

use_cases.title: Customer support and contact center troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Support teams use session replay tied to contact center interactions to quickly understand what a customer experienced before escalating or resolving an issue.
use_cases.audience: Customer support and contact center operations teams

features: Full session replay and journey analytics
Felix AI Analyst, Agent, and Partner modes
Optional add-ons: employee experience monitoring, data enrichment, contact center integration, data warehouse streaming

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, cross-platform IRC client built around a distributed core-and-client architecture, so your connection to IRC networks stays alive and your chat history persists even when your device is offline.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source under the GNU General Public License (GPL-2.0 and GPL-3.0); there are no paid tiers or commercial licenses.
enrichment.company_size: Volunteer open-source project maintained by the Quassel IRC Team, not a commercial company
enrichment.headquarters: No corporate headquarters - distributed open-source community project
enrichment.seo_title: Quassel IRC Review 2026: Features, Setup and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Everything you need to know about Quassel IRC, the free open-source always-on IRC client with a core-client architecture, before you install it.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Quassel IRC is a free, open-source, cross-platform IRC client first introduced in 2008 and released under the GNU General Public License versions 2 and 3. It is developed and maintained by a volunteer community, the Quassel IRC Team, rather than a commercial company, and its source code is hosted publicly on GitHub. Quassel's defining architectural choice is a split between a lightweight core process and one or more client interfaces: the core maintains persistent connections to IRC networks, logs conversation history to a PostgreSQL or SQLite database, and keeps running whether or not a client is attached, while native Qt-based clients on Linux, macOS, and Windows (plus community mobile clients Quasseldroid for Android and iQuassel for iOS) can connect and disconnect from that core at any time without losing context, similar in spirit to running a text client inside screen or tmux but with a graphical interface. This means a user can close their laptop, reopen it hours later, and see the full conversation history that occurred while they were away, including messages from channels they were not actively viewing. Quassel supports simultaneous connections to multiple IRC networks, encrypted SSL connections between the core and its clients, customizable multi-identity management with per-network nicknames and away messages, command aliases, and proxy support. It became notable enough to be adopted as the default IRC client in Kubuntu starting with version 9.04 in 2009. The project has an active, if slower-paced, release cadence, with version 0.14.0 shipping January 1, 2022, and translation work maintained by community contributors into languages including German, French, Czech, and Finnish, with several other translations at varying levels of completeness. Because it is entirely free, open source, and self-hosted (users typically run the core themselves on a server, NAS, or always-on machine), Quassel has no company financials, funding, headquarters, or employee count to report; the project is sustained by contributor time rather than commercial revenue. It appeals most to technically comfortable IRC users, open-source community members, and system administrators who want an always-connected chat presence without paying for or relying on third-party IRC bouncer hosting services, and it is commonly compared to other IRC clients such as HexChat, irssi, and WeeChat, though Quassel is distinguished specifically by its built-in core-client split rather than requiring separate bouncer software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Quassel IRC is a free and open-source IRC client introduced in 2008 that solves a long-standing IRC problem: staying connected and not missing messages when you close your laptop or switch devices. It does this through a core-and-client architecture, where a persistent core process maintains your IRC network connections and stores chat history in a PostgreSQL or SQLite database, while separate graphical clients built with the Qt framework connect to that core whenever you want to read or send messages.

Because the core keeps running independently, Quassel behaves similarly to pairing a terminal multiplexer with a text-based IRC client, but with a full graphical interface and native apps for Linux, macOS, and Windows, along with community-maintained mobile clients for Android and iOS. The project is maintained entirely by volunteer contributors under the GPL and has no commercial entity, pricing, or company behind it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Quassel supports simultaneous connections to multiple IRC networks from a single core, SSL-encrypted connections between the core and any attached clients, and flexible multi-identity management so users can present different nicknames or away messages on different networks. Chat history is retained in the core's database, meaning conversations that happened while no client was connected are still available once you reconnect.

Additional conveniences include command aliases for frequently used IRC commands, proxy support for connecting through restricted networks, and a native, customizable Qt-based interface. The core-client split also means a single core installation can serve multiple client devices, so a user can pick up the same synchronized conversation on a desktop client and a mobile client without losing scrollback or state.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Quassel IRC is entirely free. It is distributed under the GNU General Public License version 2 and version 3, which means the source code is open, redistribution and modification are permitted under the license terms, and there is no cost to download, install, or use the software.

There are no premium tiers, subscriptions, or paid add-ons. Running Quassel does typically require hosting the core process somewhere that stays online, such as a personal server, always-on desktop, or virtual private server, which may carry hosting costs, but those costs are unrelated to the Quassel software itself, which remains free regardless of deployment.

features.title: Core-client architecture
features.description: A persistent core maintains IRC connections and history independently of any connected client, so nothing is lost when you disconnect.

features.title: Multi-network support
features.description: Connect to and manage multiple IRC networks simultaneously from a single core and interface.

features.title: Persistent chat history
features.description: Conversation logs are stored in a PostgreSQL or SQLite database on the core, available whenever you reconnect.

features.title: Cross-platform native clients
features.description: Native Qt-based clients for Linux, macOS, and Windows, plus community mobile apps Quasseldroid and iQuassel.

features.title: Encrypted core-client connections
features.description: SSL encryption secures the link between the core and any client connecting to it.

features.title: Multi-identity management
features.description: Configure distinct nicknames, away messages, and identities per network or channel.

features.title: Command aliases and proxy support
features.description: Streamline repetitive IRC commands and connect through network proxies when needed.

features.title: Open-source and free forever
features.description: Released under GPL-2.0 and GPL-3.0, with publicly available source code and no licensing cost.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Always-on core keeps chat history and presence intact across disconnects
Supports many simultaneous IRC network connections
Native cross-platform clients plus community mobile apps
SSL-encrypted core-client connections for security
Long-standing project with a stable, established codebase
cons: Requires running and maintaining a core process on an always-on machine or server
Development pace has slowed, with the last major release in January 2022
Setup is more involved than single-binary IRC clients for newcomers
User interface feels dated compared to modern chat applications
Mobile clients are community-maintained rather than official first-party apps
No built-in support for chat protocols beyond IRC

faqs.question: Is Quassel IRC free
faqs.answer: Yes. Quassel IRC is completely free and released under the GNU General Public License versions 2 and 3, with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: What platforms does Quassel support
faqs.answer: Quassel has native clients for Linux, macOS, and Windows, plus community-maintained mobile clients Quasseldroid for Android and iQuassel for iOS.

faqs.question: What is the core-client architecture in Quassel
faqs.answer: Quassel splits into a persistent core that maintains IRC connections and stores history, and separate clients that can connect or disconnect from that core at any time without losing chat state.

faqs.question: Is there a mobile app for Quassel
faqs.answer: Yes, community-maintained mobile clients exist: Quasseldroid for Android and iQuassel for iOS, both of which connect to a Quassel core.

faqs.question: Is Quassel IRC still maintained
faqs.answer: Quassel is an active open-source project with a volunteer maintainer team, though its release cadence has slowed, with version 0.14.0 released in January 2022 as the most recent major version.

faqs.question: What license does Quassel use
faqs.answer: Quassel is released under the GNU General Public License, dual-licensed as GPL-2.0-only or GPL-3.0-only.

faqs.question: How is Quassel different from other IRC clients like HexChat
faqs.answer: Unlike single-process clients such as HexChat, Quassel separates a persistent core from the client interface, so your IRC connection and history survive even when no client is attached, without needing separate bouncer software.

faqs.question: Do I need to run my own server to use Quassel
faqs.answer: Yes, Quassel requires running the core component somewhere that stays online, such as a personal server, NAS, or VPS, in addition to installing a client to connect to it.

use_cases.title: Always-connected IRC for open-source communities
use_cases.description: Developers and maintainers who participate in project IRC channels use Quassel to stay continuously connected and never miss discussion, even when switching devices.
use_cases.audience: Open-source contributors and maintainers

use_cases.title: Self-hosted alternative to bouncer-plus-client setups
use_cases.description: Users who previously ran a terminal client inside screen or tmux with a separate IRC bouncer use Quassel to get the same always-on behavior with a graphical interface built in.
use_cases.audience: Linux and Unix power users and system administrators

use_cases.title: Small team internal chat over IRC
use_cases.description: Small technical teams that standardize on IRC for internal communication use Quassel so team members share a persistent, synchronized view of channel history.
use_cases.audience: Small technical teams and hobbyist communities

features: Self-hosted core with no usage limits

enrichment.short_description: Red Hat's container image registry, available as the hosted Quay.io service and as self-managed Red Hat Quay, providing secure image storage, vulnerability scanning, geo-replication, and automated builds for Docker and OCI images.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free for public repositories on Quay.io; paid tiers for private repositories on Quay.io; Red Hat Quay self-managed enterprise deployments are sold as a custom Red Hat subscription priced per deployment.
enrichment.starting_price: Free for public repositories; private repository and enterprise pricing not publicly disclosed - contact Red Hat sales
enrichment.headquarters: Raleigh, North Carolina, USA (Red Hat headquarters)
enrichment.seo_title: Red Hat Quay Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: A practical look at Red Hat Quay and Quay.io: container registry features, security scanning, pricing, and how it compares to other registries.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Quay is a container image registry that began in 2013 as an independent hosted service offering enterprise-grade features like image rollback and zero-downtime garbage collection, among the first hosted registries of its kind. In 2014 Quay was acquired by CoreOS, which used it as the foundation for an on-premise Quay Enterprise offering in addition to the hosted Quay.io service. When Red Hat acquired CoreOS in January 2018, Quay became part of Red Hat's product portfolio and was rebranded Red Hat Quay; Red Hat subsequently open-sourced the underlying technology as Project Quay. Today Quay exists in two closely related forms: Quay.io, a free public hosted registry (with paid tiers for private repositories) run by Red Hat, and Red Hat Quay, a self-managed, subscription-based enterprise registry that organizations deploy in their own infrastructure, often alongside Red Hat OpenShift and Kubernetes. Red Hat itself is headquartered in Raleigh, North Carolina, and operates as a subsidiary of IBM following IBM's 2019 acquisition of Red Hat, with Red Hat's global workforce numbering in the tens of thousands. Quay's core value proposition is security and operational control: it includes built-in vulnerability scanning (historically via the Clair scanner), fine-grained access control through robot accounts, detailed audit logging with rollback capability, automated image builds triggered by pushes to GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket, and high-availability geo-replication so a single registry can serve multiple data centers or cloud regions behind one URL. Red Hat Quay subscriptions are priced per deployment rather than per node, meaning a single registry installation using a shared data backend counts as one deployment regardless of how many pods or replicas run within it, and pricing is available in Standard and Premium support tiers through direct engagement with Red Hat sales rather than a public price list. Quay.io's public repositories remain free to use, making it a popular home for open-source project container images, while private repository pricing on Quay.io is tiered by the number of private repositories rather than by data volume or pull count. Quay is most commonly evaluated against other container registries such as Docker Hub, Amazon ECR, Azure Container Registry, Google Artifact Registry, and Harbor, and it is frequently chosen by organizations already invested in the Red Hat and OpenShift ecosystem or those with strict security, compliance, and image-scanning requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Quay is a container image registry with roots going back to 2013, when it launched as one of the first hosted enterprise container registries, offering features like image rollback and zero-downtime garbage collection before container registries were common. CoreOS acquired Quay in 2014 and built an on-premise Quay Enterprise offering around it, and when Red Hat acquired CoreOS in January 2018, Quay became a core part of Red Hat's cloud and container portfolio under the name Red Hat Quay.

Red Hat later open-sourced the underlying technology as Project Quay, and the product now exists in two forms: the publicly hosted Quay.io service, which is free for public repositories, and self-managed Red Hat Quay, a subscription product that organizations deploy in their own environments, frequently alongside Red Hat OpenShift and other Kubernetes distributions for enterprise-grade security and control over container image supply chains.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Quay's security-first design includes built-in vulnerability scanning of container images, fine-grained access control through robot accounts intended for automated systems, and detailed audit logging that supports rolling back changes when something goes wrong. Automated build triggers connect directly to GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket so new image versions can be built and published automatically whenever code is pushed.

For organizations operating at scale, Red Hat Quay supports high availability and geo-replication, allowing a single logical registry to run across multiple data centers or cloud regions behind one URL, which is particularly valuable for globally distributed Kubernetes and OpenShift deployments. Quay supports both Docker and OCI image formats and integrates tightly with the broader Red Hat and Kubernetes ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Public repositories on Quay.io are free to host and use, which is why many open-source projects rely on it for distributing container images at no cost. Private repositories on Quay.io are sold in tiered plans based on the number of private repositories rather than data volume, though Red Hat does not publish a fixed price list for these tiers.

Self-managed Red Hat Quay for enterprise deployments is sold as a Red Hat subscription priced per deployment, where a deployment refers to a single registry installation using a shared data backend, with no limit on the number of pods that can be run within that deployment. Subscriptions are available at Standard or Premium support levels, and exact pricing requires contacting Red Hat sales for a quote based on deployment scope and support needs.

features.title: Built-in vulnerability scanning
features.description: Automatically scans container images for known vulnerabilities to help teams catch security issues before deployment.

features.title: Robot accounts and access control
features.description: Fine-grained, auditable access delegation designed for automated systems and CI/CD pipelines rather than human logins.

features.title: Automated build triggers
features.description: Builds and publishes new container images automatically in response to pushes from GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket.

features.title: Audit logging and rollback
features.description: Maintains an easy-to-audit history of registry changes with the ability to roll back to previous states.

features.title: High availability and geo-replication
features.description: Runs multiple registry instances across data centers or cloud regions behind a single URL for resilience and low-latency access.

features.title: Free public repositories on Quay.io
features.description: Hosts public container images at no cost, widely used by open-source projects for image distribution.

features.title: OCI and Docker image support
features.description: Compatible with both Docker and Open Container Initiative image formats for broad tooling compatibility.

features.title: Kubernetes and OpenShift integration
features.description: Integrates closely with Kubernetes and Red Hat OpenShift for enterprise container platform workflows.

pros: Strong built-in security scanning and audit logging out of the box
Proven at enterprise scale with roots in CoreOS and Red Hat engineering
Flexible deployment as either hosted Quay.io or self-managed Red Hat Quay
Free hosting for public repositories benefits open-source projects
Solid geo-replication and high-availability options for global deployments
Deep integration with Kubernetes and OpenShift environments
cons: Enterprise and private repository pricing is not publicly transparent
Self-managed deployment and sizing can require significant operational expertise
Smaller community mindshare compared to Docker Hub for general-purpose use
Some advanced features are gated behind a paid Red Hat subscription
Learning curve for configuring full RBAC and geo-replication correctly
Pricing model per deployment can be less intuitive than simple per-seat pricing

faqs.question: What is the difference between Quay.io and Red Hat Quay
faqs.answer: Quay.io is Red Hat's publicly hosted registry service, free for public repositories with paid tiers for private ones, while Red Hat Quay is the self-managed, subscription-based version organizations deploy in their own infrastructure.

faqs.question: Is Quay free to use
faqs.answer: Public repositories on Quay.io are free. Private repositories on Quay.io require a paid plan, and self-managed Red Hat Quay requires a Red Hat subscription.

faqs.question: Who owns Quay
faqs.answer: Quay is owned by Red Hat, which itself is a subsidiary of IBM. Quay was originally an independent product acquired by CoreOS in 2014, then became part of Red Hat when Red Hat acquired CoreOS in 2018.

faqs.question: Does Quay support Kubernetes
faqs.answer: Yes, Quay integrates closely with Kubernetes and is commonly deployed alongside Red Hat OpenShift as the container image registry for Kubernetes-based platforms.

faqs.question: What is Project Quay
faqs.answer: Project Quay is the open-source upstream project that Red Hat released, forming the technical foundation for both the hosted Quay.io service and the commercial Red Hat Quay product.

faqs.question: Does Quay scan images for vulnerabilities
faqs.answer: Yes, Quay includes built-in container image vulnerability scanning as a core security feature.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Quay
faqs.answer: Yes, Red Hat Quay can be self-hosted in your own infrastructure as a subscription product, separate from the publicly hosted Quay.io service.

use_cases.title: Secure enterprise container registry
use_cases.description: Regulated organizations use Red Hat Quay's vulnerability scanning, audit logging, and access control to meet compliance requirements for container image supply chains.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise platform and security engineering teams

use_cases.title: Multi-region container distribution
use_cases.description: Global organizations use Quay's geo-replication to serve container images with low latency to Kubernetes and OpenShift clusters across multiple regions.
use_cases.audience: Platform engineers running multi-region Kubernetes or OpenShift fleets

use_cases.title: Free public image hosting for open-source projects
use_cases.description: Open-source maintainers use Quay.io to host and distribute public container images at no cost to their user community.
use_cases.audience: Open-source project maintainers

pricing_plans.price: Not publicly disclosed - tiered by number of private repositories

enrichment.short_description: High-performance, open-source time-series database built for fast ingestion and low-latency SQL analytics on time-stamped data, commonly used for financial market data, IoT telemetry, and observability workloads.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open-core model: the self-hosted core database is free under the Apache 2.0 license; QuestDB Cloud is a usage-based managed offering, and QuestDB Enterprise is custom-priced self-hosted software with additional support and features.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source, self-hosted); QuestDB Cloud starts at approximately 272 US dollars per month
enrichment.headquarters: London, United Kingdom and New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: QuestDB Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: An independent look at QuestDB, the open-source time-series database: architecture, features, pricing tiers, and how it compares to InfluxDB.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: QuestDB is an open-source time-series database designed for very fast data ingestion and low-latency SQL query performance on time-stamped data. The company behind it was founded in 2019 by Vlad Ilyushchenko, who serves as CTO and brings roughly two decades of experience building low-latency electronic trading platforms in FX, equities, and fixed income, and Nicolas Hourcard, CEO, who previously worked at Nasdaq and co-founded a Y Combinator open-source community initiative. QuestDB is a Y Combinator-backed company with dual headquarters in London, United Kingdom, and New York, United States, and it describes itself as a small, focused engineering team, generally estimated in the range of a few dozen employees. The company has raised approximately 15 million US dollars in venture funding, including a 12 million dollar Series A round announced in November 2021 with participation from investors such as Episode 1 Ventures, Uncorrelated Ventures, and 468 Capital. Technically, QuestDB uses a column-oriented storage engine with SIMD-vectorized query execution to achieve high throughput on both writes and reads, and it supports multiple ingestion protocols including the InfluxDB Line Protocol, the PostgreSQL wire protocol, and REST APIs, making it straightforward to plug into existing observability and IoT pipelines. The database extends standard SQL with time-series-specific functions such as SAMPLE BY for downsampling and LATEST ON for retrieving the most recent readings per series, and it supports open storage formats including Parquet alongside native integrations with tools like Grafana and Prometheus. QuestDB is distributed under the permissive Apache 2.0 license for its open-source core, which can be self-hosted at no cost. For teams that do not want to operate their own infrastructure, QuestDB Cloud offers a managed, usage-based hosted version starting at roughly 272 US dollars per month, while QuestDB Enterprise adds features aimed at larger organizations such as role-based access control, TLS encryption across all protocols, data compression, cold storage tiers, replication, and prioritized support, sold as custom-priced self-hosted software. QuestDB is most commonly compared to other time-series and analytical databases such as InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, and ClickHouse, and it is particularly popular among teams working with financial market tick data, industrial IoT sensor streams, and application performance monitoring or observability metrics where both high ingest rates and fast SQL analytics are required simultaneously.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: QuestDB is an open-source time-series database purpose-built for workloads that generate enormous volumes of time-stamped data and need both extremely fast ingestion and low-latency SQL analytics, such as financial market data, industrial sensor telemetry, and application observability metrics. The company was founded in 2019 by Vlad Ilyushchenko, whose background is in building low-latency electronic trading systems, and Nicolas Hourcard, formerly of Nasdaq, and it operates as a Y Combinator-backed startup with headquarters spanning London and New York.

QuestDB's core differentiator is combining the raw ingest and query performance associated with specialized time-series engines with a familiar, standard SQL interface extended with time-series-specific functions, so teams do not need to learn a proprietary query language to get the performance benefits of a purpose-built engine.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Under the hood, QuestDB uses a column-oriented storage engine paired with SIMD-vectorized query execution to process large volumes of data quickly, and it supports flexible ingestion through the InfluxDB Line Protocol, the PostgreSQL wire protocol, and REST endpoints, so it can slot into existing data pipelines without requiring a proprietary client.

QuestDB extends SQL with time-series-native functions like SAMPLE BY for time-bucketed aggregation and LATEST ON for retrieving the most recent value per series, and it supports open data formats such as Parquet alongside native integrations with common observability tools like Grafana and Prometheus. QuestDB Enterprise builds on the open-source core with role-based access control, TLS everywhere, data compression, cold storage tiering, and replication for organizations with stricter operational and compliance requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The QuestDB core database is fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to self-host, with no artificial feature limits placed on the open-source version for single-node deployments. This makes it accessible to individual developers and teams who are comfortable managing their own infrastructure.

For teams that prefer a managed service, QuestDB Cloud is a usage-based hosted offering that starts at approximately 272 US dollars per month depending on instance size and usage. Organizations with larger-scale or compliance-driven needs can opt for QuestDB Enterprise, a custom-priced self-hosted subscription that adds high availability, RBAC, TLS across all protocols, and dedicated support, with exact pricing available directly from QuestDB.

features.title: Column-oriented storage engine
features.description: Optimized for time-series workloads with fast writes and efficient scans across large historical datasets.

features.title: SIMD-vectorized query execution
features.description: Uses vectorized processing to accelerate SQL query performance on large volumes of time-stamped data.

features.title: Multiple ingestion protocols
features.description: Supports InfluxDB Line Protocol, PostgreSQL wire protocol, and REST APIs for flexible data ingestion.

features.title: Time-series SQL extensions
features.description: Adds functions like SAMPLE BY and LATEST ON to standard SQL for downsampling and retrieving latest readings per series.

features.title: Open storage format support
features.description: Works with open formats such as Parquet, avoiding vendor lock-in for stored data.

features.title: Native observability integrations
features.description: Connects directly with tools like Grafana and Prometheus for dashboards and monitoring.

features.title: QuestDB Enterprise security features
features.description: Adds role-based access control, TLS encryption across all protocols, and data compression for larger deployments.

features.title: High availability and replication
features.description: Enterprise tier supports replication and cold storage tiering for resilience and cost-efficient long-term retention.

pros: Very fast ingestion and query performance for time-series-specific workloads
Free and open source under the permissive Apache 2.0 license
SQL-native interface lowers the learning curve for existing SQL users
Supports multiple ingestion protocols for easy integration into existing pipelines
Backed by Y Combinator and an experienced founding team from trading and Nasdaq backgrounds
Active open-source community and public GitHub development
cons: Smaller plugin and integration ecosystem compared to established players like InfluxDB or TimescaleDB
Enterprise features such as HA, RBAC, and replication require a paid tier
QuestDB Cloud pricing beyond the starting tier requires a custom quote
Best suited specifically to time-series workloads rather than general-purpose relational use
Small company size relative to larger established database vendors
Documentation and community resources are still maturing compared to older databases

faqs.question: Is QuestDB free
faqs.answer: Yes, the core QuestDB database is open source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to self-host. QuestDB Cloud and QuestDB Enterprise are paid managed and enterprise offerings respectively.

faqs.question: What is QuestDB used for
faqs.answer: QuestDB is used for time-series workloads that need fast ingestion and low-latency SQL queries, such as financial market data, IoT sensor telemetry, and application observability metrics.

faqs.question: Who created QuestDB
faqs.answer: QuestDB was founded in 2019 by Vlad Ilyushchenko (CTO) and Nicolas Hourcard (CEO), and the company is backed by Y Combinator.

faqs.question: What license does QuestDB use
faqs.answer: QuestDB's open-source core is released under the Apache 2.0 license.

faqs.question: How does QuestDB compare to InfluxDB
faqs.answer: Both are time-series databases, but QuestDB emphasizes a standard SQL interface with time-series extensions and column-oriented, SIMD-accelerated query execution, while InfluxDB uses its own query languages and storage engine.

faqs.question: Does QuestDB support SQL
faqs.answer: Yes, QuestDB uses standard SQL extended with time-series-specific functions like SAMPLE BY and LATEST ON.

faqs.question: Is there a managed cloud option for QuestDB
faqs.answer: Yes, QuestDB Cloud is a managed, usage-based hosted version of QuestDB starting at approximately 272 US dollars per month.

enrichment.seo_title: QuestionPro Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how QuestionPro's survey builder, pricing tiers, and CX and research suites compare before you choose a survey platform in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: QuestionPro is an online survey and research platform founded in 2005 that has grown from a straightforward survey builder into a broader suite of research, customer experience, and employee experience products. The company began in Seattle, later moved its headquarters to San Francisco, and relocated its global headquarters to Austin, Texas, in 2020 to support business growth and talent recruiting. QuestionPro reports roughly 200 to nearly 500 employees across offices in the United States, India, Mexico, the United Arab Emirates, and Germany, serving customers worldwide. The platform's core product is a drag-and-drop survey builder supporting more than 40 question types, branching and skip logic, offline data collection for field research, and advanced analytics with cross-tabulation and dashboard reporting. Beyond core surveys, QuestionPro offers specialized suites: a Research Suite for market researchers and panel management, a CX suite for customer experience and Net Promoter Score programs, and a Workforce or Employee Experience suite for HR teams running engagement and pulse surveys. A standout capability is QuestionPro's multilingual support: the platform interface itself is localized into more than 230 languages, while survey content and responses can be translated and collected in more than 90 to roughly 95 languages, including multi-byte character languages like Chinese and Japanese and right-to-left languages such as Hebrew and Arabic, with both automatic one-click translation and professional translation services available. On pricing, QuestionPro offers a genuinely free Essentials plan aimed at individuals and small projects, which is more generous than many competing survey tools' free tiers. Paid plans scale by user count, with a Team Edition starting at roughly 83 US dollars per user per month when billed annually for organizations with five or more users, and an Advanced tier at roughly 99 US dollars per user per month; large organizations needing the Research, CX, or Workforce suites are quoted custom enterprise pricing. QuestionPro also offers discounted rates for academic institutions and non-profit organizations. The company is commonly compared to other survey and experience management platforms such as SurveyMonkey, Qualtrics, and Typeform, and it is frequently chosen by academic researchers, market research agencies, and enterprise CX and HR teams that need deep multilingual reach, offline data collection, and specialized reporting beyond what simpler survey tools provide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: QuestionPro is an online survey and research platform founded in 2005 that has expanded well beyond basic survey creation into a family of specialized products covering market research, customer experience, and employee experience. Originally started in Seattle, the company later moved its headquarters to San Francisco before relocating its global headquarters to Austin, Texas, in 2020, and it now operates offices across the United States, India, Mexico, the United Arab Emirates, and Germany.

The platform is used by a wide range of customers, from individual researchers and students on its free tier to large enterprises running dedicated market research, customer experience, and workforce engagement programs through QuestionPro's specialized suites, which sit alongside its core survey-building tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its foundation, QuestionPro offers a drag-and-drop survey builder with more than 40 question types, branching and skip logic, and offline data collection for field-based research where internet access is unreliable. Reporting tools include cross-tabulation, advanced analytics, and customizable dashboards for turning raw responses into actionable findings.

One of QuestionPro's most distinctive strengths is language reach: the platform interface is localized in more than 230 languages, and survey content can be translated and collected in roughly 90 to 95 languages, including multi-byte character set languages like Chinese and Japanese and right-to-left languages such as Arabic and Hebrew, using either one-click automatic translation or professional translation services. Beyond core surveys, dedicated suites address market research and panel management (Research Suite), customer experience and NPS tracking (CX), and employee engagement (Workforce or EX).

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: QuestionPro offers a genuinely usable free Essentials plan intended for individuals and small, quick research projects, which is more feature-rich than many competitors' free tiers. Paid plans are priced per user: the Team Edition starts at roughly 83 US dollars per user per month when billed annually for organizations with five or more users, while the Advanced tier runs approximately 99 US dollars per user per month.

For organizations that need the specialized Research Suite, CX suite, or Workforce and Employee Experience suite, QuestionPro moves to custom enterprise pricing based on scope and requirements, quoted directly by its sales team. The company also offers discounted pricing for academic institutions and registered non-profit organizations.

enrichment.short_description: Intuit's QuickBooks accounting software brand, spanning the QuickBooks Desktop product line (Pro Plus, Premier Plus, and Enterprise) used by small and midsize businesses for bookkeeping, invoicing, payroll, and financial reporting.
enrichment.pricing_model: Annual desktop software subscriptions sold per license/user for QuickBooks Desktop products (Pro Plus, Premier Plus, Enterprise), separate from Intuit's cloud QuickBooks Online and QuickBooks Time monthly subscriptions.
enrichment.starting_price: From approximately 1,149 US dollars per year for QuickBooks Desktop Pro Plus (single user, as of February 2026 pricing)
enrichment.seo_title: QuickBooks Review 2026: Desktop Pricing, Plans and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: An independent guide to the QuickBooks Desktop product line: Pro Plus, Premier Plus, and Enterprise pricing, features, and what replaced discontinued plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: QuickBooks is the accounting software brand created by Intuit, the financial software company founded in 1983 by Scott Cook and Tom Proulx and headquartered in Mountain View, California. QuickBooks itself launched in 1992 as a DOS-based small business accounting program, quickly followed by Windows and Mac versions, and by the mid-2000s it had come to dominate the small business accounting software market in the United States, reportedly holding around 74 percent market share by 2005 and roughly 94 percent by 2008. While the QuickBooks name today is most associated with the cloud-based QuickBooks Online product, the QuickBooks brand also encompasses the original QuickBooks Desktop product line, which remains in active use by many established small and midsize businesses and accounting firms, particularly those with complex, industry-specific, or highly customized bookkeeping workflows. The Desktop lineup historically included QuickBooks Pro, Premier, and Enterprise editions; Intuit stopped selling new QuickBooks Desktop Pro Plus, Premier Plus, and Mac Plus subscriptions to new customers after September 30, 2024, though existing subscribers can continue to renew their licenses. QuickBooks Enterprise remains open to new customers and is the only Desktop edition still actively sold to newcomers, though at a significantly higher price point that now includes a per-employee payroll fee. Desktop pricing rose sharply on February 1, 2026: single-user Pro Plus and Mac Plus increased from 999 to 1,149 US dollars per year, Premier Plus single-user pricing rose from 1,399 to 1,609 US dollars per year with additional multi-user seats increasing from 300 to 345 US dollars per user, and Enterprise pricing now starts around 2,210 US dollars per year for the Gold tier, roughly 2,717 US dollars per year for Platinum, and custom pricing above 4,200 US dollars per year, billed monthly, for the Diamond tier. Support timelines are also shifting: QuickBooks Desktop 2023 support ends May 31, 2026, after which payroll, bank feeds, and security updates stop functioning, while Desktop 2024 remains supported until September 30, 2027. Intuit, the parent company, employs roughly 18,200 people globally, operates more than 80 offices across 14 countries, and reported trailing twelve-month revenue of roughly 20.9 billion US dollars as of April 2026 across its full portfolio, which includes TurboTax, Credit Karma, QuickBooks, and Mailchimp in addition to QuickBooks. Intuit's active product development has clearly shifted toward QuickBooks Online, and Desktop customers are increasingly being encouraged to migrate to the cloud product, though Desktop editions, especially Enterprise, remain relevant for businesses with established desktop workflows, industry-specific edition needs (such as construction, manufacturing, retail, or nonprofit), or environments where a locally installed application is preferred over a browser-based tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: QuickBooks is the accounting software brand built by Intuit, the company founded in 1983 by Scott Cook and Tom Proulx and headquartered in Mountain View, California. QuickBooks launched in 1992 as DOS-based small business accounting software, expanded to Windows and Mac shortly afterward, and became the dominant small business accounting platform in the United States by the mid-2000s.

While QuickBooks Online has become Intuit's primary focus for new development, the QuickBooks brand also includes the original QuickBooks Desktop product line covering Pro Plus, Premier Plus, and Enterprise editions, which remains actively used by established small and midsize businesses, particularly those needing industry-specific editions or locally installed software rather than a browser-based tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: QuickBooks Desktop products offer double-entry bookkeeping, invoicing and estimates, bill payment and expense tracking, inventory management in Premier and Enterprise editions, and industry-specific editions of Enterprise built for sectors like construction, manufacturing, retail, and nonprofits. Desktop editions also support multi-user network hosting, detailed financial reporting, 1099 contractor tracking, and integration with Intuit's payroll and merchant payment services.

QuickBooks Enterprise, the only Desktop edition still sold to new customers, adds advanced inventory, more granular user permissions, and industry-specific reporting suited to larger and more complex small businesses. Across the QuickBooks brand, Intuit also maintains a large ecosystem of certified ProAdvisors, accountants, and third-party integrations built around QuickBooks data files and formats.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: QuickBooks Desktop is sold as annual subscriptions rather than the perpetual one-time licenses of the pre-2022 era. As of February 2026 pricing, single-user Pro Plus and Mac Plus cost 1,149 US dollars per year, up from 999 US dollars, while Premier Plus single-user pricing rose to 1,609 US dollars per year, with additional multi-user seats priced at 345 US dollars per user.

New Desktop customers can now only purchase QuickBooks Enterprise, which starts around 2,210 US dollars per year for the single-user Gold tier, approximately 2,717 US dollars per year for Platinum, and custom pricing above roughly 4,200 US dollars per year for the Diamond tier, billed monthly and including a per-employee payroll fee. Existing Pro Plus and Premier Plus subscribers can continue renewing even though new purchases were discontinued after September 30, 2024.

features.description: Extensive built-in reports covering profit and loss, balance sheets, and custom financial statements.

Only QuickBooks Enterprise remains available for new purchases, at a much higher price point

Older Desktop versions lose support on a rolling schedule, requiring periodic paid upgrades
Multi-user and per-employee payroll fees can add significant cost for growing teams

faqs.question: Is QuickBooks Desktop still available
faqs.answer: QuickBooks Desktop Pro Plus and Premier Plus are no longer sold to new customers as of after September 30, 2024, though existing subscribers can renew. QuickBooks Enterprise remains available for new purchases.

faqs.question: What is the difference between QuickBooks and QuickBooks Online
faqs.answer: QuickBooks refers to Intuit's broader accounting software brand, including the locally installed QuickBooks Desktop product line, while QuickBooks Online is the separate cloud-based, browser-accessible version with its own distinct pricing tiers.

faqs.question: Who owns QuickBooks
faqs.answer: QuickBooks is owned and developed by Intuit, a financial software company founded in 1983 and headquartered in Mountain View, California.

faqs.question: When was QuickBooks founded
faqs.answer: QuickBooks was first launched by Intuit in 1992 as DOS-based small business accounting software, following Intuit's founding in 1983.

faqs.question: Can I still buy QuickBooks Pro
faqs.answer: No, new purchases of QuickBooks Pro Plus were discontinued after September 30, 2024. Existing Pro Plus subscribers can continue to renew, but new customers can only purchase QuickBooks Enterprise.

faqs.question: What is QuickBooks Enterprise
faqs.answer: QuickBooks Enterprise is the most advanced Desktop edition of QuickBooks, offering industry-specific versions, advanced inventory, and more granular permissions, and it is the only Desktop edition still sold to new customers.

faqs.question: Does QuickBooks Desktop get replaced by QuickBooks Online
faqs.answer: Intuit's active product development has shifted toward QuickBooks Online, and the company is encouraging Desktop customers to migrate, though Desktop editions, especially Enterprise, remain supported and sold.

enrichment.short_description: Intuit's cloud-based accounting software for small and midsize businesses, offering invoicing, expense tracking, and financial reporting from any browser or the mobile app, sold in Solopreneur, Simple Start, Essentials, Plus, and Advanced tiers.
enrichment.pricing_model: Monthly SaaS subscription with five tiers scaling by features and user count, sold directly by Intuit with an introductory discount for new subscribers before standard pricing applies.
enrichment.starting_price: From 20 US dollars per month (Solopreneur); core QuickBooks Online plans start at 38 US dollars per month (Simple Start)
enrichment.seo_title: QuickBooks Online Pricing 2026: Plans, Tiers and What You Get
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare QuickBooks Online's Solopreneur, Simple Start, Essentials, Plus, and Advanced plans, current prices, and what each tier actually includes.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: QuickBooks Online is Intuit's cloud-based, browser-accessible accounting software for small and midsize businesses, distinct from the locally installed QuickBooks Desktop product line. Intuit first launched a cloud version of QuickBooks in 2001 to offset declining packaged desktop software sales at brick-and-mortar retailers, though early versions were limited compared to the desktop product; QuickBooks Online was substantially rebuilt in 2013, which marked the real turning point in its adoption and functionality, and it has since become Intuit's primary focus for accounting product development, with QuickBooks Desktop Pro and Premier discontinued for new purchases after September 2024. QuickBooks Online is organized into five subscription tiers as of mid-2026: Solopreneur at 20 US dollars per month for freelancers and sole proprietors needing basic income, expense, and tax tracking; Simple Start at 38 US dollars per month, which adds full invoicing, sales tax automation, bill management, and 1099 contractor tracking for a single user; Essentials at 75 US dollars per month, which adds time tracking and supports up to three users; Plus at 115 US dollars per month, aimed at growing businesses that need inventory tracking, project profitability tracking, purchase orders, budgeting, and class or location tracking, supporting up to five users; and Advanced at 275 US dollars per month, designed for established small and midsize businesses needing more users, higher transaction volumes, complex custom reporting, and stronger internal controls. QuickBooks Online has a history of raising prices roughly once a year, typically during the summer, with recent increases in the range of 12 to 17 percent annually since 2023; new subscribers typically receive an introductory discounted rate locked in for the first several months before standard pricing applies. As a division of Intuit, which is headquartered in Mountain View, California, and employs roughly 18,200 people globally with more than 80 offices across 14 countries, QuickBooks Online benefits from Intuit's broader financial technology ecosystem, including integrated payroll, payments, and third-party app marketplace connections, and it is by far the most widely used cloud accounting platform among small businesses in the United States. It is most commonly compared to competing cloud accounting products such as Xero, FreshBooks, and Wave, and it is generally chosen over QuickBooks Desktop by businesses that want anywhere, any-device access, automatic updates, and continuous feature development rather than a periodically upgraded locally installed application.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: QuickBooks Online is Intuit's cloud-based accounting product, accessible from any web browser or the QuickBooks mobile app rather than installed locally on a single machine. Intuit first introduced a cloud version of QuickBooks in 2001, though the early product was limited compared to the desktop software; a substantial rebuild in 2013 turned QuickBooks Online into the fully featured, actively developed product it is today, and it is now Intuit's primary focus for accounting software development.

Unlike QuickBooks Desktop, which is licensed annually per installation, QuickBooks Online is a straightforward monthly SaaS subscription with automatic updates, anywhere access, and tiers that scale by feature set and number of users rather than by installed workstation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: QuickBooks Online covers core small business accounting needs including invoicing, expense and bill tracking, bank feed reconciliation, sales tax automation, and 1099 contractor tracking, available even at the entry-level Simple Start tier. Higher tiers add time tracking (Essentials), inventory management, project profitability tracking, purchase orders, budgeting, and class or location tracking (Plus), and more advanced custom reporting, higher user limits, and stronger internal controls (Advanced).

Because it runs in the cloud, QuickBooks Online integrates natively with Intuit's broader ecosystem, including QuickBooks Payments, QuickBooks Payroll, and a large marketplace of third-party app integrations, and it supports real-time collaboration between business owners, employees, and outside accountants or bookkeepers without needing to share a local company file.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: QuickBooks Online is priced across five tiers: Solopreneur at 20 US dollars per month for freelancers, Simple Start at 38 US dollars per month for single-user small businesses, Essentials at 75 US dollars per month supporting up to three users with time tracking, Plus at 115 US dollars per month supporting up to five users with inventory and project tracking, and Advanced at 275 US dollars per month for larger businesses needing more users, higher transaction volumes, and advanced reporting.

QuickBooks Online has historically raised prices roughly once a year, typically in the summer, with recent annual increases ranging from about 12 to 17 percent since 2023, and new subscribers commonly receive a discounted introductory rate for the first several months before the standard monthly price takes effect.

features.title: Cloud-based access from any device
features.description: Full accounting functionality accessible from a web browser or mobile app, with no local installation required.
